criteria: The agent has read Section 1 completely to understand draft policy misalignment and its two failure modes: greedy path pruning and verification mismatch.

The agent has read Figure 2 to understand the empirical evidence of draft policy misalignment: greedy path prune rates (19–34%), greedy path accept rates (36–49%), and the gap between greedy path acceptance length (3–4 tokens) and full draft tree acceptance length (5–6 tokens).
The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the Draft Tree Reward: draft tree construction, per-sequence expected acceptance length computation, and log-sum-exp aggregation.

The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand Group Tree Optimization: two-phase training, grouping of adjacent positions, reward debiasing, group advantage standardization, and PPO-style clipped surrogate loss.

The agent has read Algorithm 1 completely to understand the full GTO Phase II training loop: group sampling, tree construction for current and reference models, reward computation, debiasing, standardization, likelihood ratio calculation, and parameter update.

The agent has read Section 4 completely to understand experiment setup: the five target models, three benchmarks (MT-Bench, HumanEval, GSM8K), all baselines, training datasets (ShareGPT and OpenThoughts-114k-math), and evaluation metrics (SR and τ).

The agent has read Appendix B completely to understand the full training configuration: draft tree structure, optimizer settings, learning rate schedule, mixed-precision settings, ZeRO configuration, gradient accumulation, epochs, and sequence length.
The agent has read Table 1 to check the main comparison results of GTO against all baselines across all five target models, three benchmarks, and two decoding temperatures.
The agent has read Tables 3 to 6 to understand the four ablation study designs: aggregation operator, group size, reward debiasing, and continual training baseline.
The agent has read Appendix E to understand the tree configuration ablation study that varies inference-time tree depth and total token budget.
The plan includes implementing draft tree construction using EAGLE-2-style layer-wise expansion with per-layer top-k selection and global top-g re-ranking and pruning.
The plan includes computing the per-sequence expected acceptance length by accumulating target model token probabilities along each draft sequence (Equations 3–4).
The plan includes aggregating sequence-level expected acceptance lengths into a single tree-level reward using log-sum-exp with temperature η (Equation 5).
The plan includes a two-phase training procedure: Phase I initializes with a pretrained draft model (e.g., EAGLE-3), and Phase II performs group-based optimization of the draft tree reward.
The plan includes partitioning training sequence positions into K non-overlapping groups of m adjacent indices (Equations 6–7).
The plan includes constructing reference model trees with the frozen reference draft model M0 and computing debiased rewards by subtracting reference model tree rewards (Equation 9).
The plan includes computing group-standardized advantages by normalizing debiased rewards within each group using within-group mean and standard deviation (Equation 10).
The plan includes computing the per-token geometric mean likelihood ratio between the current and reference draft models on the longest accepted sequence (Equation 11).
The plan includes applying the PPO-style clipped surrogate loss over each group using likelihood ratios and standardized advantages (Equation 12).
The plan includes combining the GTO loss with a token-level cross-entropy distillation loss weighted by scalar ω (Equation 13).

The plan includes main experiments testing GTO on all five target models (LLaMA-3.1-8B, Vicuna-1.3-13B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B, LLaMA-3.3-70B, Qwen3-8B) across MT-Bench, HumanEval, and GSM8K at temperatures T=0 and T=1.
The plan includes compatibility experiments applying GTO on top of GRIFFIN- and HASS-initialized draft models on LLaMA-3-Instruct-8B and LLaMA-2-Chat-7B.
The plan includes ablation studies for the tree reward aggregation operator (LSE vs. Max vs. Sum), group size m, and reward debiasing.

The plan includes training configuration from Appendix B.3: AdamW with β1=0.9, β2=0.95, learning rate 5×10^-6 with 1000-step linear warmup over 60000 total steps, gradient clipping 0.5, FP16 mixed precision, ZeRO Stage-2, 5 epochs, max sequence length 2048.

The code for draft tree construction following EAGLE-2-style layer-wise expansion (global top-k selection of frontier token edges per layer) and global top-g re-ranking with pruning of leaves has been implemented (Equation 1).
The code for representing each root-to-leaf path in the draft tree as a candidate sequence S_{t,i} of variable length l_i has been implemented (Equation 2).
The code for computing the expected acceptance length L_{t,i} per draft sequence as the sum of cumulative target model token probabilities has been implemented (Equation 3).
The code for computing the cumulative product of target model token probabilities for each token position in a draft sequence has been implemented (Equation 4).
The code for the log-sum-exp (smooth max) tree reward aggregation with temperature parameter η over all N candidate sequences has been implemented (Equation 5).
The code for partitioning sequence positions into K non-overlapping groups of m adjacent indices with the non-overlap constraint has been implemented (Equations 6–7).
The code for constructing a draft tree at each position i in a group using the current draft model M has been implemented (Equation 8).
The code for constructing reference trees at each group position using the frozen reference draft model M0 has been implemented.
The code for debiased reward computation by subtracting the reference model's tree reward from the current model's tree reward at matched prefixes has been implemented (Equation 9).
The code for group-level advantage standardization using within-group mean subtraction and standard deviation normalization with numerical stability floor δ has been implemented (Equation 10).
The code for identifying the longest accepted sequence S_hat_i in the current draft tree under the target model has been implemented.

The code for computing the per-token likelihood ratio as the exponential of the normalized log-probability difference between current and reference draft models on the longest accepted sequence has been implemented (Equation 11).

The code for the PPO-style clipped surrogate objective that takes the minimum of the unclipped and clipped (by ε) likelihood-ratio-weighted advantage, averaged over each group, has been implemented (Equation 12).
The code for the token-level cross-entropy distillation loss between the draft model and target model next-token distributions over all supervised positions has been implemented (Appendix B.2).
The code for the combined training objective L = L_token + ω · L_GTO with scalar weight ω has been implemented (Equation 13).

The complete GTO Phase II training loop (Algorithm 1) — group sampling, tree construction for M and M0, reward computation, debiasing, advantage standardization, likelihood ratio, clipped loss, and parameter update — has been implemented.

The Phase I draft model initialization — loading a pretrained EAGLE-3 (or GRIFFIN/HASS) draft model as both the starting point for the current model M and the frozen reference model M0 — has been implemented.
The draft tree configuration has been set to a fixed budget of 60 draft tokens, maximum tree depth of 7, and per-node expansion top-k of 10 (Appendix B.1).
The GTO training hyperparameters have been configured as: K=16 groups per sequence, group size m=8, smooth-max temperature η=1, and GTO loss weight ω=0.5 (Appendix B.3).

The AdamW optimizer has been configured with β1=0.9, β2=0.95, weight decay=0, linear learning rate warmup to 5×10^-6 over 1000 steps then decay over 60000 total steps, and gradient clipping at 0.5 (Appendix B.3).

The training precision and parallelism has been configured: FP16 mixed precision with dynamic loss scaling, ZeRO Stage-2 with overlapping communication, gradient accumulation of 2 steps, per-GPU micro-batch size 1 (Appendix B.3).
The training loop parameters have been configured: 5 epochs, max sequence length 2048, and 2 dataloader workers (Appendix B.3).
The training data loading has been implemented for the ShareGPT dataset (all models) and the OpenThoughts-114k-math dataset (additionally for DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B).
The code for loading and configuring all five target models (LLaMA-3.1-Instruct-8B, Vicuna-1.3-13B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B, LLaMA-3.3-Instruct-70B, Qwen3-8B) has been implemented.
The speculative decoding evaluation pipeline measuring speedup ratio (SR) and acceptance length (τ) at batch size 1 under decoding temperatures T∈{0,1} has been implemented.

The inference code for all baseline methods (PLD, Lookahead, SPS, Medusa, Hydra, EAGLE, EAGLE-2, HASS, GRIFFIN, EAGLE-3) has been implemented using their public implementations and official decoding hyperparameters.
The ablation variants for Sum (average) and Max aggregation operators in the tree reward have been implemented as alternatives to the default LSE aggregation.
The ablation variant without reward debiasing — omitting the reference model tree reward subtraction in Equation 9 — has been implemented.
The continual training baseline — extending EAGLE-3 training on the same ShareGPT data with the original EAGLE-3 training objective for 200 additional A100-80G GPU hours — has been implemented.
GTO Phase II training has been executed successfully without errors on all five target models using their respective training datasets.
The main comparison experiments have been executed successfully for all baselines and GTO on LLaMA-3.1-8B across MT-Bench, HumanEval, and GSM8K at T=0 and T=1.
The main comparison experiments have been executed successfully for Vicuna-1.3-13B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B, LLaMA-3.3-70B, and Qwen3-8B across all benchmarks and temperatures.
The compatibility experiments have been executed successfully, fine-tuning GRIFFIN- and HASS-initialized draft models with GTO on LLaMA-3-Instruct-8B and LLaMA-2-Chat-7B.
The aggregation operator ablation has been executed successfully, comparing LSE, Max, and Sum variants on LLaMA-3.1-8B across all three benchmarks at T=0 and T=1.
The group size ablation has been executed successfully, testing m∈{1, 4, 8, 16, 32} on LLaMA-3.1-8B across all benchmarks at T=0 and T=1.
The reward debiasing ablation has been executed successfully, comparing GTO with and without the reference model reward subtraction on LLaMA-3.1-8B across all benchmarks.
The continual training ablation has been executed successfully, comparing GTO, original EAGLE-3, and the continual training baseline on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B.
The tree configuration ablation has been executed successfully, testing depth∈{6, 7, 8} combined with total token budget∈{50, 60, 70} on LLaMA-3.1-8B.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: GTO achieves average SR=3.73× and τ=6.52 on LLaMA-3.1-8B at T=0, outperforming EAGLE-3 (SR=3.46×, τ=6.07).
The execution outcomes match Table 1: GTO achieves average SR=5.61× and τ=7.29 on Vicuna-1.3-13B at T=0, outperforming EAGLE-3 (SR=5.11×, τ=6.80).
The execution outcomes match Table 1: GTO achieves average SR=3.89× and τ=6.37 on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B at T=0, outperforming EAGLE-3 (SR=3.57×, τ=5.79).

The execution outcomes match Table 1: GTO consistently outperforms all baselines across all five target models, three benchmarks, and both temperatures, improving average SR over EAGLE-3 by 7.7% at T=0 and 5.6% at T=1.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: GRIFFIN+GTO improves average SR by 7.8% and τ by 7.4% over the GRIFFIN baseline at T=0 on LLaMA-3-Instruct-8B.
The execution outcomes match Table 2: HASS+GTO improves average SR by 8.3% and τ by 8.0% over the HASS baseline at T=0 on LLaMA-3-Instruct-8B.
The execution outcomes match Table 3: LSE aggregation (GTO) achieves the best average SR=3.73 and τ=6.52 at T=0 on LLaMA-3.1-8B, outperforming Max (SR=3.65, τ=6.40) and Sum (SR=3.55, τ=6.24).
The execution outcomes match Table 4: Group size m=8 achieves the best average SR=3.73 at T=0, with degradation at m=1 (SR=3.60), m=16 (SR=3.55), and m=32 (SR=3.44), and m=4 within 1% of m=8.
The execution outcomes match Table 5: GTO with reward debiasing outperforms the w/o debiasing variant by +5.0% SR and +5.1% τ at T=0 on LLaMA-3.1-8B.
The execution outcomes match Table 6: GTO outperforms both the original EAGLE-3 (SR=3.89 vs. 3.57 at T=0) and the continual training baseline (SR=3.89 vs. 3.60) on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B.
The execution outcomes confirm Figure 2(a): EAGLE-3 on LLaMA-3.1-8B exhibits greedy path prune rates of 19–34% and greedy path accept rates of only 36–49% across MT-Bench, HumanEval, and GSM8K.

The execution outcomes confirm Figure 2(b): accepted greedy paths average only 3–4 tokens while the full draft tree achieves 5–6 tokens average acceptance length, confirming the sub-optimality of greedy-path training.

The execution outcomes match Table 7: GTO maintains strong performance across all nine tree configurations (depth∈{6,7,8} × total tokens∈{50,60,70}), with speedup varying only 1%–3% from the optimal depth=7, tokens=60 setting.
